docs.title: Modernism & Literature
docs.text:
Welcome to the course website. Here you will find:

  • 📚 Readings for each week
  • 📝 Assignments and guidelines
  • 📑 Lecture slides and supporting materials

Instructor: Prof. [Your Name] Semester: Fall 2025 Contact: your.email@university.edu

docs.title: Course Description
docs.text: This course explores literary modernism through key texts, critical essays, and historical context.

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text: By the end of the semester, students will: 1. Analyze major modernist texts. 2. Relate literature to its historical and cultural contexts. 3. Produce original critical writing.

docs.title: Weekly Schedule
docs.text: Week Topic Reading 1 Introduction to Modernism Week 1 Readings 2 T.S. Eliot & Early Poetry Week 2 Readings

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Write a 1,200–1,500 word essay analyzing one text from Weeks 1–3, focusing on how it embodies (or resists) modernist aesthetics.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Include at least two critical sources.
  • Cite all references using MLA style.
  • Submit via email as a PDF by Oct 12, 2025.

docs.title: Suggested Topics
docs.text:
• The role of time in Woolf’s fiction
  • The city in early modernist poetry
  • Intertextuality in Eliot’s early work

docs.title: Required Reading
docs.text:
• Virginia Woolf, Modern Fiction (1925)
  • Ezra Pound, A Few Don’ts by an Imagiste (1913)

docs.title: Supplementary
docs.text:
• Lecture 1 Slides
  • Short video: What is Modernism? (link to YouTube)

docs.title: Discussion Questions
docs.text:
1. How do Woolf and Pound define “modern” literature?
  2. What challenges do they pose to traditional narrative form?

docs.title: Required Reading
docs.text:
• T.S. Eliot, The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock (1915)
  • Selected criticism on Eliot’s early work

docs.title: Discussion Questions
docs.text:
1. How does Eliot use fragmentation and juxtaposition in Prufrock?
  2. What is gained and lost by the poem’s “free verse” style?
